questions.text: In cash receipts fraud terminology, what does 'skimming' specifically refer to?
options: A. The theft of cash before it is recorded in the accounting system
B. The alteration of sales invoices to reduce the recorded amount
C. The unauthorized use of company credit cards for personal expenses
D. The manipulation of bank reconciliations to hide missing deposits

questions.text: Which of the following best defines 'lapping' in the context of cash receipts fraud?
options: A. Recording multiple small transactions as one large transaction to avoid detection
B. Using subsequent customer payments to cover the theft of earlier payments
C. Creating fictitious customer accounts to generate false revenue
D. Transferring funds between bank accounts to conceal unauthorized withdrawals

questions.text: In asset misappropriation schemes, what is 'cash larceny' precisely defined as?
options: A. The theft of cash after it has been recorded in the accounting system
B. The unauthorized removal of inventory valued at cost
C. The creation of false invoices to generate phantom sales
D. The redirection of customer payments to personal bank accounts

questions.text: What does the term 'unrecorded sale' refer to in cash receipt fraud schemes?
options: A. A sale that is recorded but the customer is not billed for the merchandise
B. A transaction where cash is received but not entered into the sales system at all
C. A sale recorded at a different price than what the customer actually paid
D. A transaction that appears in accounts receivable but has no corresponding cash receipt

questions.text: In the context of cash receipts fraud, what is specifically meant by 'write-off fraud'?
options: A. Removing legitimate receivables from the books to conceal the theft of their payments
B. Creating deductions on tax returns that are not properly documented
C. Recording fictitious expenses to increase deductible business losses
D. Adjusting inventory records to reflect obsolete merchandise

questions.text: What does 'account receivable manipulation' specifically involve in cash misappropriation schemes?
options: A. Increasing the credit limit granted to favored customers
B. Altering customer account records to mask the theft of their payments or balances
C. Creating duplicate invoices to the same customer within the same billing period
D. Reducing the allowance for doubtful accounts to inflate reported profits

questions.text: In cash receipt schemes, what is the technical definition of 'register disbursement fraud'?
options: A. An employee removes recorded cash from the register and creates a false expense voucher
B. A manager approves unauthorized refunds or discounts that are not processed through normal channels
C. The cashier intentionally gives incorrect change to customers and pockets the difference
D. An employee skims cash before the register is reconciled at end of day

questions.text: Which term best describes the practice of reducing recorded cash receipts through false discounts or allowances that benefit the perpetrator?
options: A. Fictitious returns
B. Unauthorized credit memos
C. Sales suppression
D. Refund manipulation

questions.text: In cash receipts fraud, what does 'sales suppression' specifically mean?
options: A. The intentional underreporting of total sales revenue by failing to record some transactions
B. The postponement of customer invoicing to a future accounting period
C. The cancellation of customer orders due to inventory unavailability
D. The reduction of sales commissions paid to employees

questions.text: What is the precise definition of 'forced balance' technique in cash receipt schemes?
options: A. Manipulating bank reconciliations to make discrepancies artificially disappear
B. Forcing customers to pay outstanding balances before making new purchases
C. Creating offsetting entries to hide stolen cash from audit procedures
D. Requiring daily cash counts to be equal to recorded receipts regardless of actual cash on hand

questions.text: In the context of asset misappropriation, what specifically distinguishes 'refund fraud' from other cash receipt schemes?
options: A. It involves the creation of fictitious customers to generate false refunds
B. It specifically targets the processing of legitimate or fraudulent refunds to accomplish theft
C. It requires manipulation of the accounts receivable aging report
D. It involves the alteration of original sales invoices before processing refunds

questions.text: What does the term 'phantom credit' mean in cash receipts fraud terminology?
options: A. A credit card used by an employee without authorization from the cardholder
B. A false credit applied to a customer account to conceal the theft of their actual payment
C. A promotional discount that was never approved by management
D. A duplicate credit memo issued for the same transaction

questions.text: In cash misappropriation schemes, what is 'check diversion' precisely defined as?
options: A. Redirecting customer payments by check to an unauthorized bank account controlled by the perpetrator
B. Forging customer signatures on checks intended for the company
C. Depositing company checks into personal accounts before they are recorded
D. Delaying the deposit of customer checks to create float for personal use

questions.text: A perpetrator in a cash receipts fraud scheme deposits customer payments into a personal bank account rather than the company's account. Which type of asset misappropriation does this represent?
options: A. Skimming before the money enters the accounting system
B. Cash larceny after the transaction is recorded
C. Lapping scheme involving multiple customer accounts
D. Register disbursement fraud with falsified documentation

questions.text: When distinguishing between skimming and cash larceny in a retail environment, which characteristic is unique to cash larceny?
options: A. The theft occurs before any accounting entry is made
B. The perpetrator must manipulate existing accounting records to conceal the theft
C. Customer payments are never recorded in the sales journal
D. The scheme involves writing off receivables as bad debt

questions.text: An accounts receivable clerk at a manufacturing company receives customer checks and has the ability to record payments in the accounting system. To conceal theft, the clerk applies subsequent payments to accounts that were previously skimmed. What is this technique called?
options: A. Cash larceny rotation scheme
B. Lapping or payment rotation scheme
C. Register manipulation with false credits
D. Unrecorded sales concealment

questions.text: Which control is most effective at preventing a situation where an employee receives cash from customers but fails to record the transaction in the accounts receivable system?
options: A. Segregating the duties of cash receipt, deposit preparation, and accounts receivable recording
B. Implementing a cash register that prints transaction receipts
C. Requiring monthly reconciliation of bank statements
D. Conducting surprise cash counts at random intervals

questions.text: A CFE is investigating a healthcare provider where the billing department manager can approve write-offs of patient balances up to $5,000. The CFE notices that numerous balances just under this threshold have been written off to bad debt, coinciding with missing cash receipts. Which fraud indicator is most relevant here?
options: A. Unexplained increases in accounts receivable aging
B. Abuse of authority to conceal cash theft through inappropriate write-offs
C. Systematic lapping of payments across multiple patient accounts
D. False recording of sales transactions at reduced amounts

questions.text: In distinguishing between skimming schemes, what is the defining characteristic of an unrecorded sales scheme compared to a receivables skimming scheme?
options: A. Unrecorded sales skimming involves theft before any transaction is documented, while receivables skimming occurs after a sale is recorded
B. Unrecorded sales skimming requires lapping, while receivables skimming does not
C. Unrecorded sales skimming affects only cash accounts, while receivables skimming affects both cash and receivables
D. Unrecorded sales skimming involves customer refunds, while receivables skimming involves payment reversals

questions.text: A retail store's point-of-sale system shows that one cashier has significantly higher voided transaction rates than peers. Upon investigation, the CFE discovers the cashier is processing refunds to non-existent customers and pocketing the cash. Which cash receipts fraud scheme does this represent?
options: A. Sales skimming with fictitious refund documentation
B. Register disbursement fraud using false refund transactions
C. Cash larceny disguised as legitimate refund processing
D. Lapping scheme applied to refund accounts

questions.text: When comparing payment application fraud to lapping schemes, what is the primary distinction?
options: A. Payment application fraud involves stealing one customer's payment and applying another's payment to conceal it, while lapping is identical in execution
B. Payment application fraud requires multiple perpetrators, while lapping requires only one employee
C. Payment application fraud involves applying a current payment to a previous receivable to hide theft, while lapping specifically involves a continuous rotation across many accounts
D. Payment application fraud is a one-time event, while lapping always involves systemic patterns

questions.text: A CFE examining a small business discovers that the owner's family member receives customer payments and deposits them into a personal account weekly. The amounts are then transferred to the company account days later. Which internal control weakness is most apparent?
options: A. Failure to segregate the duties of cash receipt, custody, and recording
B. Lack of surprise cash audits by independent third parties
C. Absence of a formal cash application policy
D. Inadequate review of aged accounts receivable

questions.text: In an accounts receivable fraud investigation, the CFE must distinguish between a scheme where an employee steals a customer's payment and subsequently applies that same customer's next payment to mask the theft, versus a scheme involving multiple customer accounts in sequence. What is the correct terminology for these schemes respectively?
options: A. Both are examples of lapping schemes with no distinction
B. Single-account skimming versus multi-account lapping
C. Direct cash theft versus receivable manipulation fraud
D. Payment diversion versus systematic lapping

questions.text: An employee at a shipping company collects COD (Cash on Delivery) payments from customers but only records a portion in the company's system, keeping the difference. Later, when the customer complains, the employee applies a subsequent COD payment from another delivery to the missing amount. Which two schemes are occurring sequentially?
options: A. Skimming followed by lapping
B. Lapping followed by skimming
C. Register disbursement followed by cash larceny
D. Unrecorded sales followed by payment reversal

questions.text: A CFE is comparing two potential cash receipts fraud scenarios: Scenario 1 involves an employee who voids transactions and pockets the cash, and Scenario 2 involves an employee who steals customer payments before they are recorded. What is the key difference in detection difficulty between these schemes?
options: A. Scenario 1 is easier to detect because voided transactions create audit trails, while Scenario 2 is harder because no initial record exists
B. Scenario 2 is easier to detect because customer complaints arise quickly, while Scenario 1 is concealed by system documentation
C. Both scenarios are equally difficult to detect without surprise audits
D. Scenario 1 is impossible to detect because the company expects zero revenue from voided transactions

questions.text: When evaluating a suspected cash receipts fraud in a law firm's client trust account, the CFE discovers that the trust accounting clerk can receive client payments, record them in the trust ledger, and transfer balances between client accounts. Which control deficiency specifically enables lapping schemes in this environment?
options: A. The absence of a monthly bank reconciliation by a third party
B. Lack of segregation between receipt authorization, recording, and custody of cash
C. The trust account's exemption from external audit requirements
D. Insufficient documentation of client payment sources

questions.text: A manufacturing company's receiving department accepts shipments and logs them into the inventory system. The accounts payable clerk then matches invoices to receiving reports before processing payments. A CFE discovers that a receiving clerk has been accepting deliveries that were never ordered, creating fictitious receiving reports. Which preventive control would most effectively stop this scheme?
options: A. Requiring the accounts payable clerk to verify all receiving reports independently
B. Implementing a three-way match procedure that compares purchase orders, receiving reports, and supplier invoices before payment
C. Increasing the frequency of physical inventory counts to detect discrepancies
D. Requiring supervisor approval only for invoices exceeding a certain dollar threshold

questions.text: During a forensic investigation of a retail store, a CFE notices that the night shift cashier consistently has cash overages at the end of shift, while the day shift cashier frequently has shortages. When the CFE reviews transaction logs, both cashiers process similar volumes. What is the most likely explanation for this pattern?
options: A. The night shift cashier is more skilled at cash handling and makes fewer mistakes
B. The night shift cashier is skimming cash during the day shift by falsifying the previous cashier's count
C. The day shift cashier is conducting a cash larceny scheme by pocketing legitimate receipts and later manipulating the cash count
D. The store's cash register system has a software malfunction that only affects the day shift transactions

questions.text: A hotel front desk manager has authority to issue room refunds up to $500 without additional approval. A CFE's analysis reveals that this manager issued 47 refunds in the past month, averaging $498 each, totaling $23,406. Guest records show no complaints corresponding to most of these refunds. What fraud technique is most likely occurring?
options: A. Skimming of advance guest deposits before they are recorded in the system
B. Structured refunds deliberately kept just below the approval threshold to avoid detection
C. Collusion with the accounting department to reverse legitimate charges from the system
D. Unauthorized cash disbursements disguised as legitimate guest refunds

questions.text: A CFE is investigating a medical billing department where patient payments are received by mail and processed by a billing clerk. The CFE notices that some patient accounts show full payments, but the corresponding deposits are less than the total payments received. Which control weakness most directly enabled this situation?
options: A. Failure to segregate the duties of receiving payments, recording payments, and depositing funds
B. Inadequate training of billing staff on proper cash handling procedures
C. Insufficient reconciliation of patient statements to aging reports
D. Lack of management review of daily cash deposit amounts

questions.text: An audit of a convenience store reveals that a cashier has been conducting a skimming scheme for six months. The perpetrator's method was to not ring up selected cash sales on the register and pocket the cash immediately. During the investigation, the CFE reviews the inventory records and finds that inventory levels have remained consistent despite the missing sales. How is this possible?
options: A. The perpetrator was replacing skimmed inventory with personal cash to cover the shortfall
B. The store's inventory system was not updated to reflect the skimmed sales, so the records appeared artificially accurate
C. The skimming amounts were too small to create a measurable impact on inventory turnover ratios
D. The perpetrator was selectively skimming only high-margin items that were manually counted rather than system-tracked

questions.text: A law firm receives client retainer payments that are initially deposited into a client trust account before being transferred to the operating account as services are rendered. A partner has been transferring retainer funds to the operating account before any services are provided and using the money for firm operating expenses. Which fraud classification best describes this scheme?
options: A. Skimming, because the cash is not recorded as revenue when received
B. Cash larceny, because the perpetrator is taking funds that have already been recorded in the trust account
C. Embezzlement, because the perpetrator is misappropriating funds entrusted to the firm
D. Register manipulation, because the perpetrator is falsifying the transfer records between accounts
questions.tag: Cash Larceny vs. Embezzlement—Recording Status Distinction

questions.text: During a surprise audit of a nonprofit organization's donation receipts, a CFE discovers that a development officer has been receiving donations, issuing receipts to donors, but not depositing all funds. The CFE calculates that approximately $18,000 in donations over three months were not deposited. When confronted, the officer claims the money was for 'emergency expenses' and promises to repay it. Based on fraud theory, what element of the fraud triangle was most critical in this case?
options: A. Pressure, as the officer may have faced personal financial difficulties
B. Opportunity, as the officer had sole access to donation processing without oversight
C. Rationalization, as the officer attempted to justify the theft as temporary borrowing
D. Motive, as the nonprofit's financial situation created desperation among staff
questions.tag: Fraud Triangle—Opportunity in Cash Receipt Functions

questions.text: A restaurant employs a host/cashier who seats customers, processes payments, and maintains the reservation book. A CFE suspects this employee is conducting a skimming scheme and wants to design a detection test. Which analytical procedure would be most effective?
options: A. Compare the number of reservation entries to the number of cash transactions recorded for each shift
B. Analyze the ratio of cash payments to credit card payments to identify unusual patterns
C. Review employee scheduling records to identify shifts with the highest revenue variance
D. Calculate the average table turnover rate and compare it to expected customer counts based on inventory of ingredients used

questions.text: A manufacturing company's accounts receivable department discovered that a clerk had been applying payments from new customers to old outstanding invoices without customer authorization. When the actual payers inquired about their account status, the clerk claimed the older invoices belonged to 'related accounts' that would be consolidated later. Which red flag should have been caught earlier in this lapping scheme?
options: A. The presence of frequent journal entries reversing old receivable balances
B. A pattern of customers disputing their account balances because payments were not applied as instructed
C. An unusually high number of old invoices being written off as uncollectible in a short period
D. Differences between the accounts receivable aging report and the general ledger control account balance
questions.tag: Lapping Scheme—Fraud Indicators and Customer Disputes

questions.text: A parking garage operates with an automated payment system for daily parkers and a separate manual collection system for monthly pass holders. A CFE is investigating suspected cash receipts fraud and learns that the maintenance worker, who empties the cash box weekly, has been diverting approximately 15% of cash receipts. The CFE wants to determine how long this scheme could have continued undetected. Which factor would be most significant in allowing extended concealment?
options: A. The maintenance worker's long tenure with the company, which created trust among supervisors
B. Lack of reconciliation between the automated system's electronic records and the actual cash collected
C. The maintenance worker's authority to approve manual adjustments to monthly pass holder accounts
D. Insufficient segregation of duties in the accounting department's cash recording function

questions.text: A CFE reviewing a retail chain's cash receipt procedures identifies that store managers have authority to issue 'no-sale' adjustments on registers without requiring supporting documentation. These adjustments are used when a customer returns merchandise without a receipt. Over the past year, one store manager issued $34,000 in no-sale adjustments compared to an average of $8,200 across other stores of similar size. What is the most appropriate investigative step?
options: A. Immediately file criminal charges against the manager for probable fraud
B. Obtain samples of no-sale adjustments, match them to returned merchandise, and interview customers to verify legitimacy
C. Review the manager's personnel file to identify any prior disciplinary issues
D. Implement a policy eliminating all no-sale adjustments effective immediately

questions.text: A university's student payment system allows students to pay tuition online, by check, or in cash at the cashier's office. A CFE discovers that a cashier has been recording payments as received but directing students to mail checks to a PO box in the cashier's name rather than the university. The checks were deposited into the cashier's personal account, and the cashier would later deposit personal funds into the university account to cover the stolen amounts. Which aspect of this scheme makes detection particularly difficult?
options: A. The perpetrator was skimming small amounts that were difficult to track individually
B. The university's accounting records showed all payments as received and reconciled to deposits, masking the fraud
C. The online payment system was not integrated with the cash office, creating separate records
D. The perpetrator had authority to write off uncollectible accounts, which could have concealed shortfalls
questions.tag: Fraud Concealment—Reconciliation Manipulation Through Compensating Transactions

questions.text: A CFE is designing an internal control system for a startup company that processes credit card payments for e-commerce transactions. The company wants to minimize fraud risk while avoiding excessive control costs. Which single control would provide the most effective protection against cash receipt misappropriation in this environment?
options: A. Requiring daily reconciliation of the payment processor's settlement report to the accounting system and investigating all variances immediately
B. Implementing encryption of all customer payment information stored in the company's database
C. Conducting monthly surprise audits of the payment processing department's records
D. Training all employees on fraud prevention and requiring them to sign a code of conduct

questions.text: A manufacturing company's accounts receivable clerk has the ability to receive customer payments, post them to the general ledger, and approve write-offs of uncollectible accounts. During a forensic investigation, a CFE discovers that the clerk has been intercepting checks from a major customer, depositing them into a personal account, and then writing off the customer's account as uncollectible after 90 days. Which control deficiency most directly enabled this fraud scheme?
options: A. Lack of segregation of duties between cash receipt authorization and posting to accounts receivable
B. Absence of independent reconciliation of the accounts receivable subsidiary ledger to the general ledger
C. Failure to implement dual custody requirements for customer remittance notifications
D. Inadequate review of employee transactions by senior management on a periodic basis

questions.text: During the investigation of a retail chain, a CFE identifies that a cashier has been using a systematic method where legitimate customer payments are recorded in the point-of-sale system, but the cash is diverted before it reaches the deposit bag. When the store manager reconciles the register tape to the cash drawer at shift end, the shortage is covered by the cashier using personal funds, which are later withdrawn as legitimate change. Which type of cash receipts fraud is most accurately described by this scenario?
options: A. Skimming with temporary cash substitution
B. Cash larceny with compensating manipulation
C. Register disbursement with fraudulent refund processing
D. Unrecorded sales with deferred reconciliation evasion

questions.text: A CFE is examining a hotel's front desk operations where the night auditor has access to the reservation system, can void room charges, and reconciles the daily cash report. Over six months, the auditor has voided charges for high-value rooms before guests checked out, then pocketed the cash payments made upon arrival. The voiding created no reconciliation issues because the auditor also controls the daily report. What is the primary forensic indicator that would most effectively detect this specific scheme?
options: A. Comparison of voided transactions against actual guest departures using hotel management system data
B. Analysis of the night auditor's personal bank deposits correlated with high-value room bookings
C. Review of the authorization logs showing which employees voided which transactions
D. Examination of discrepancies between the point-of-sale revenue and the accounts receivable aging

questions.text: In a medical billing fraud investigation, a CFE discovers that an employee processes patient payments and has been applying incoming checks from insurance companies to older outstanding patient balances rather than to the current period's claims. This allows the employee to create fictitious refunds to patients for overpayments that never occurred, processing the refund checks through a personal account. Which term most precisely describes the temporal manipulation component of this fraud?
options: A. Circular lapping with synthetic refund generation
B. Accounts receivable manipulation through temporal misalignment
C. Deferred lapping with false overpayment claim creation
D. Sequential skimming with retroactive account reconciliation

questions.text: A CFE is investigating a collection agency where the cashier receives payments from debtors, records them in the system, and prepares daily deposits. The investigator notices that deposits are consistently 3-5% lower than recorded receipts. Upon further inquiry, the CFE learns that the cashier has been removing a portion of cash receipts daily, but maintains the accounting records as if the full amount was deposited. The cashier explains that she planned to repay the amounts once her personal financial situation improved. From a forensic perspective, which aspect of this scheme presents the greatest ongoing detection challenge?
options: A. The perpetrator's intent to repay makes this a civil matter rather than criminal fraud
B. The consistent percentage variance creates a predictable pattern that facilitates reconciliation matching
C. The daily removal of funds requires the perpetrator to maintain the fraudulent scheme continuously without break
D. The cashier's access to both receipt documentation and deposit records enables easy concealment

questions.text: During an audit of a nonprofit organization's donation processing, a CFE discovers that a development officer receives donor checks, issues receipts, and deposits funds into the organization's account. An analysis of donor correspondence reveals that four major donors made pledges totaling $250,000, but the organization's bank deposits for the corresponding period show only $175,000 received. The development officer claims some donors have not yet fulfilled their pledges. However, the CFE finds bank statements from the donors showing they mailed checks covering the full pledge amounts. Which investigative procedure would most definitively establish whether skimming has occurred?
options: A. Request copies of the original pledge agreements and compare against cancelled checks from donor banks
B. Analyze the development officer's personal financial records for deposits matching the variance amount
C. Review email communications between the development officer and donors regarding pledge fulfillment
D. Examine the organization's bank reconciliation statements for the time period in question

questions.text: A restaurant chain's loss prevention team notices that one cashier's drawer consistently balances perfectly at shift end, while other cashiers average a 0.8% variance. Further investigation reveals that this cashier achieves perfect balancing by using a personal float of approximately $200-$300 daily, which is carefully managed but never properly documented. When interviewed, the cashier states this practice helps prevent shortages. Under forensic examination, which aspect of this situation presents the most significant concern for potential fraud?
options: A. The perfect reconciliation indicates the cashier has superior cash handling skills compared to peers
B. The undocumented personal float creates an opportunity for the cashier to obscure small daily skimming amounts
C. The variance among other cashiers suggests systematic register malfunctions rather than employee dishonesty
D. The consistent float amount indicates the cashier is following informal but acceptable practices

questions.text: A CFE is reviewing a law firm's trust account procedures where client funds are received by the receptionist, recorded in a trust ledger by the accounting manager, and deposited by the office administrator. During a surprise audit, the CFE reconciles client ledger balances to actual trust account balances and discovers a $85,000 discrepancy that cannot be explained by timing differences. The receptionist and accounting manager deny knowledge of the variance, but the office administrator cannot be located. Subsequent investigation reveals the administrator has been intercepting client checks before they are recorded, depositing them into the trust account, but then processing unauthorized transfers to a related entity controlled by a family member. Which control would have been MOST effective in preventing this specific scheme?
options: A. Requiring the receptionist to prepare a daily list of received checks that the accounting manager independently verifies against deposits
B. Implementing a policy requiring all trust account transfers to be approved by at least two authorized signatories
C. Installing video surveillance of the area where client payments are initially received and logged
D. Rotating the office administrator's responsibilities monthly to different staff members

questions.text: During a forensic examination of a casino's cage operations, a CFE identifies that a cage cashier has developed a scheme where players requesting cash-out of their gaming credits receive the requested amount, but the cashier records the transaction at a slightly lower amount in the system. The difference is pocketed by the cashier. Over eight months, this scheme generated approximately $12,000 in fraudulent extractions. When discovered, the cashier's defense includes evidence that the discrepancies fall within the expected variance parameters that management had previously established as acceptable without investigation. Which professional standard should guide the CFE's conclusion regarding materiality in this case?
options: A. The absolute dollar amount of $12,000 should be deemed immaterial due to the casino's large revenue base
B. The established variance thresholds should be applied uniformly to determine if the scheme is material
C. The repetitive nature and intentional misrecording of transactions indicates materiality regardless of management's variance policy
D. The cashier's position in the organization determines the materiality threshold for evaluating the fraud

questions.text: A CFE is investigating suspected cash receipt fraud at a utility company where customers make payments through multiple channels: in-person at payment centers, by mail, through automatic bank transfers, and via an online portal. An analysis of the accounts receivable aging shows that customers making in-person payments have a significantly higher rate of disputed charges than customers using other payment methods. Further analysis reveals that a payment center employee has been processing customer overpayments without issuing formal refund checks, instead instructing customers that credits would be applied to future bills. Which analytical procedure would most effectively quantify the magnitude of potential skimming through this specific method?
options: A. Recalculate customer accounts receivable balances based on original payment amounts and compare to recorded amounts
B. Analyze the variance between actual customer complaints filed and the historical baseline complaint rate
C. Compare the number of in-person customer payments to online payments for the investigation period
D. Review the employee's work schedule against days when the highest volumes of customer disputes were recorded

questions.text: A financial institution's CFE discovers that a loan officer has been intercepting customer loan payment checks, endorsing them with a rubber stamp bearing the bank's name, and depositing them into a personal account at another financial institution before the loan payment is recorded in the system. The loan officer then processes the loan as 30+ days past due and contacts the borrower threatening collection action, while simultaneously accepting partial payments from the borrower that are immediately diverted. This multi-layered scheme continued for 18 months before detection. Which internal control implementation would be LEAST effective at preventing this particular scheme?
options: A. Requiring dual signature authorization on all checks received for deposit
B. Implementing automated reconciliation of customer payments to loan account postings on a daily basis
C. Prohibiting employees from having personal accounts at the same financial institution
D. Installing a policy requiring customers to be notified electronically whenever a payment is recorded to their account

questions.text: During a forensic investigation of a county government tax collection office, a CFE discovers a sophisticated lapping scheme where property tax payments are systematically applied to older accounts rather than current accounts. The scheme was made possible because the tax assessor position has remained vacant for 14 months, creating a significant backlog of property transactions, account modifications, and payment reconciliations. The perpetrator exploited this ambiguity to maintain the scheme while appearing to be working through the backlog. Which aspect of this case best illustrates a common environmental precondition for cash receipt fraud?
options: A. Inadequate training of accounting personnel in lapping detection techniques
B. Extended periods of insufficient staffing creating opportunities for reduced oversight and transaction concealment
C. Outdated information technology systems that cannot process payment applications efficiently
D. Excessive workload demands on individual employees reducing their conscientious attention to detail

questions.text: A CFE examining a property management company's cash receipt procedures discovers that tenant rent payments received in person are recorded in the property management software by the on-site office manager, who also maintains the physical cash and prepares deposits. The company's policy requires monthly reconciliation of recorded collections to bank deposits, but this reconciliation is performed by the same office manager. During a surprise cash count, the CFE finds that the physical cash on hand exceeds the recorded collections for the month by $3,200. When questioned, the office manager explains that this represents advance rent payments from new tenants that have not yet been recorded because the lease documents are still being finalized. However, the CFE discovers that the office manager has been receiving advance rent payments but deliberately delaying their recording to create a hidden cash reserve that is withdrawn periodically for personal use. Which red flag most clearly indicates intentional fraud rather than mere accounting delay?
options: A. The lease documents being incomplete, which is a normal part of the tenant move-in process
B. The timing of the advance rent receipts correlating with the office manager's known personal financial difficulties
C. The office manager's explanation being plausible and consistent with standard property management practices
D. The amount of unrecorded receipts remaining relatively small compared to total monthly collections
